data.title: HelloFresh: Efficiency Gains Mask A Business Without A Growth Engine
data.description:
I last covered HelloFresh SE (HLFFF, HELFY) in July 2024, arguing the company faced a fundamental scalability issue. My thesis was that HelloFresh relied on generous marketing expenses to grow their user base but failed to convert customers to long-term users at a satisfying rate. I bet that, long term, the company would have either managed to fix this structural issue or pivoted to a new business model.

Today, almost two years after my first coverage, I am reviewing HelloFresh again to assess whether their business model is sustainable and an investment case can be made.

A flawed business model in two charts

My bearish thesis for HelloFresh is fairly simple: the company relies on generous handouts to acquire new customers and retain existing ones, but customers do not actually remain loyal to the company to a satisfying degree (i.e., the churn rate is too high).

The two charts below, which I updated from my 2024 coverage, show marketing expenses and operating income for HelloFresh on an annual basis. At that time, based on 2023 data, I argued what follows:

The trend, in my opinion, is clear: while HelloFresh is indeed managing to scale its business, it does so primarily via handouts to acquire and retain customers. The company almost tripled in revenue since 2020, but it has done so by disproportionately increasing its marketing expenses. As a result, HelloFresh has been unable to steadily grow its Operating Income.

Two years later, the picture is bittersweet and somewhat bearish.

On the positive side, HelloFresh did manage to slow down their marketing expenses, which have stabilized at around ~€1.4 billion for the past three years.

But at what cost?

The company's revenue (not shown in these charts) has also stalled at about ~€7.9 billion (slightly decreasing since its 2023 peak).

More worryingly, operating income has also stalled, and the company has been roughly breaking even in both 2024 and 2025.

In other terms, the company stopped growing their customer base (and revenue) as soon as their marketing spend stalled, proving my original point.

I see this situation as proof the business model of HelloFresh is fundamentally flawed long term. Customers are simply not sticking with a meal-kit subscription model without being given handouts, or not at a pace that makes such a business sustainable long term.

The consumer insight behind a flawed business model: convenience

From a consumer standpoint, the idea that a meal kit service is not financially sustainable has some relatively straightforward grounds.

People are generally willing to pay for convenience when it comes to food and food delivery. Businesses like DoorDash, Inc. (DASH), have been able to scale back their marketing expenses and climb to profitability over the years. They managed to "hook" people on the convenience of having food delivered to their door, even well after the pandemic made this a necessity.

By contrast, delivering meal kits that still need to be cooked at home significantly decreases the convenience element. It is then little surprise that consumers are unwilling to stay with such a subscription model long term.

A flawed business warranted a pivot, turning out to be worse than the original

A flawed business model per se would not warrant a bearish thesis on HelloFresh. Any company with good enough management can successfully recover from a weak core business by pivoting to a more profitable one.

In this regard, I find it very indicative how Blue Apron, the second-largest meal-kit delivery service in the US, recently pivoted their business model. The company is moving from subscriptions to on-demand orders of meal kits, further adding two new product lines that directly address the convenience consumer element:

  • "Dish by Blue Apron," consisting of ready-to-eat, nutrition-focused meals that only need to be reheated.

  • "Assemble & Bake," which are meal kits coming together all on one sheet pan (significantly reducing the "cooking" element).

HelloFresh also followed a similar pivot, pushing RTE (ready-to-eat meals), a product line acquired in 2020 under the brand "Factor". However, this pivot has so far been a disappointment, with management mentioning in the most recent earnings call:

In [RTE], we absorbed the consequences of regulatory-driven food manufacturing challenges that cascaded into product quality issues and impacted customer experience at exactly the wrong time, while we were simultaneously spending on brand and performance marketing. The result was worse unit economics, lower marketing ROI and lower customer retention, resulting in a significant gap to our original plan.

In fact, HelloFresh's sluggish performance in 2025 was mostly driven by the RTE (ready-to-eat) segment, with a full-year RTE AEBITDA margin at -1.2%.

Blue Apron (which is part of Wonder Group) is not listed anymore, and there is limited data available for this company. Therefore, we cannot possibly know whether their own pivot was more successful from a financial standpoint.

Ultimately what matters in the context of my thesis is that finding new, more profitable ventures seems a necessity for all companies in this industry.

Management talks about an 'efficiency reset': is this enough?

Given the secular weakness of "meal-kit-as-a-service" and the failure of RTE, the big question is what is management doing to address their profitability issues?

In the latest earnings reports available, management mentions "efficiency resets" as a key focus area. The narrative they are proposing is that of a cyclical business that is undergoing a stagnation phase (with weak demand). Management expects demand to turn positive again in "18 to 20 months" and mentions they are focusing on efficiencies in the meantime (see chart below).

And efficiencies have indeed been delivered. For the full year 2025, HelloFresh marketing spend was cut by over €203 million versus 2024. In meal kits specifically, the year-over-year percentage reduction in marketing spend was double the rate of order decline. US meal kit marketing spend fell more than 40% in USD terms in 2025. This, together with other efficiency measures, contributed to making the core business of meal kits financially sustainable.

However, I will be honest and say I am not reassured by this overall narrative in the slightest. Even if I were to believe that meal kits are going to come back in fashion (which I do not), we are talking about what today is a declining industry. And this is acknowledged by HelloFresh, as shown in the slide below. Overall, meal kits have been in decline, while a core base of "tenured" customers has remained highly stable. Clearly, though, this base has not been enough to bring the company where it needs to be in terms of profitability.

The reason management is focusing on meal kits is fairly easy to understand: the pivot to RTE has not worked and ended up hindering the company's financial performance. But this, in my view, should not be a reason to double down on a legacy business that has been stagnant for a couple of years. Even if the core business may be profitable in itself, I do not think investing in a slowly decaying business that depends on efficiencies to operate can ever constitute a compelling investment case.

In this regard, I really think that HelloFresh needs to either show that RTE can be brought to growth or find yet another business to pivot to.

Why a 'Hold': HelloFresh would trade at ~8x P/E without RTE

In writing this article, I hesitated between a SELL and a HOLD rating until the last moment. On the one hand, this is a barely profitable business that has seen declining revenues for three years now. Management has also failed in their pivot to RTE, and they are somewhat doubling down on their meal kits again, providing questionable guidance for a return to growth in 2027.

However, I ultimately decided that a HOLD rating is more appropriate. The reason is that if we strip down the RTE business from HelloFresh, the underlying business is profitable and very cheaply valued.

HelloFresh reports AEBITDA margins at the segment level in the deck, which allows it to back-calculate revenues precisely. The table below extrapolates data for the meal kit business from financial figures reported in euros.

Data in Million Euros

Assumptions for the above extrapolation are as follows:

  • Holding costs and "others" fully allocated to meal kits (conservative, since some would disappear without RTE complexity)

  • D&A allocated proportionally (~69% of group's total)

  • SBC kept in full (concentrated in holding)

  • Net interest proportionally adjusted

At its current market capitalization of ~€600 million (and €3.78 per share on the German stock exchange), the hypothetical valuation of HelloFresh if it only sold meal kits would be at a ~7.7X P/E. A fair valuation, in my view, warrants a HOLD.

Further risks and considerations for investors

HelloFresh's business is strictly tied to consumer sentiment. A future US and/or global recession may hinder management's idea that meal-kit demand should go back to growth in 18 to 20 months. It may also permanently depress long-term demand for a business that, for the past few years, has proven not to be sticky in terms of consumer habits. What worked in favor of the company during the pandemic may turn into a secular tailwind in case of a recession.

On the other side, if macro conditions improve quickly, the bottom for the stock may already be in as the business re-accelerates towards sustained profitability.

In this sense, investors should be aware that HelloFresh is a company that is fairly sensitive to changes in macroeconomic conditions.

Finally, it remains to be seen what direction management is going to take in the future months. Should they decide to enter a new vertical with promising growth potential and executional excellence, a BUY rating may be warranted.

Conclusion: HelloFresh cannot focus on meal kits long-term

Despite failing to grow its bottom or top line, HelloFresh at least managed to make its core business of meal-kit delivery profitable. The company did fail to execute a pivot to RTE, but management now has more breathing space to figure out their next move.

For a long-term investment case, I think we will need to see either of the following:

  • A successful turnaround of the RTE business, to the point this can be seen as a credible, future lever for growth.

  • The company entering a new adjacent space or industry altogether and either scrapping their RTE plans or bringing them (at minimum) to break-even.

Overall, I am not convinced yet by what I saw during the latest earnings. Management so far seems to want to double down on meal-kit delivery. But focusing on a declining meal-kit business that centers on "tenured customers" at the expense of volume cannot warrant a BUY case.

The market's reaction after earnings speaks for itself: the company experienced a steep sell-off after earnings, and it is now down ~40% YTD and ~95% from its all-time high.

For investors having HelloFresh in their portfolios, I think a 'Hold' can make sense to see whether the company can successfully reinvent itself, given the core business is profitable. Others may want to stay out of HelloFresh for the time being.

data.title: USD Comes Back Bid After Yesterday's Exaggerated Slide
data.description:
The market seemed to overreact to the central bank meetings this week. The market heard Fed Chair Powell as more hawkish than the FOMC statement and took the dollar sharply higher. Yesterday, it overreacted to the Bank of England and European Central Banks and sold the greenback aggressively. The swaps market is discounting three rate hikes this year by the ECB and BOE and about three basis points of tightening by the Federal Reserve.

Still, after yesterday’s sell-off, the dollar has bounced back. The fog of war seems to contribute to the desire for short-term market participants not wanting to be short dollars into the weekend. Even though the US and Israel say that they will not strike Iranian oil infrastructure, there is little sign of de-escalation, and yesterday, US Treasury Secretary Bessent made a reference to the possibility that Kharg Island could be taken over by the US. Adding to the mix is today’s “triple-witching” that see a relatively large $5.7 trillion of options on individual stocks, indices and exchange-traded funds expire. This comes amid a further sell-off in stocks and bonds.

Prices

G10

• After practically no follow-through euro selling yesterday and the sharp drop during Fed Chair Powell’s press conference on Wednesday, the euro rallied strongly yesterday. It reached a six-session high, almost $1.1615. ECB President Lagarde seemed to take a wait-and-see stance and expressed no sense of urgency, but unnamed hawks played up the risk of a hike as early as next month, and today the Bundesbank’s chief made the same point. And it is not just one hike, but now the swaps are pricing in nearly three hikes before the end of the year. However, with the weekend at hand, given the fog of war, the short-term market does not want to be short dollars. The euro has held below $1.16 today and was sold in the $1.1535 area ahead of the North American session. This risks a test on $1.15, where options for 1.5 bln euros expire today.

• The yen rose to a seven-session high yesterday as the greenback unwound the Powell-inspired gains. The dollar rose to within a whisker of JPY160 on Wednesday and early Thursday before being sold to JPY157.50 in the North American afternoon. The 20-day moving average, which the dollar has not traded below since February 24 (~JPY157.75 today), was frayed. However, market seemed to overreact yesterday, and with Tokyo markets closed for the national holiday, the greenback recovered to around JPY158.90 today. There are $920 mln options at JPY158.50 that expire today.

• Sterling also recovered smartly yesterday. After it slipped below $1.3250 in the early going, it poked above $1.3465, its best level since March 10. It took out the 20-day moving average (~$1.3405) for the first time since the middle of last month on a set of five-session highs that frayed the 20-day moving average (~$1.3400 today). There was no follow-through sterling buying today, and it mostly held below $1.3440 so far today. Nearby support is around $1.3345-55. The swaps market swung from practically flat Wednesday to pricing in an 80% chance of a hike by the Bank of England next month. Before the war, the market had a cut in April fully discounted. Three hikes before the end of the year are fully discounted.

• The Canadian dollar was the weakest currency in the G10 yesterday. It was practically flat. It was the first session since late January that the US dollar (DXY) did not trade below CAD1.3700. It is holding above CAD1.37 so far today in quiet turnover. While the greenback made a marginal new two-and-a-half-week high yesterday, it stalled near CAD1.3750, while the month’s high was a little above it. It is not clear that the consolidation pattern has ended. There are nearly $1 bln roughly divided between three strikes, CAD1.3725, CAD1.3740, and CAD1.3750 expiring today.

• The Australian dollar held $0.7000 and recovered to almost nearly $0.7110 in the North American afternoon yesterday. Tuesday and Wednesday’s highs were around $0.7120-25. It is trading a bit heavier today and has been sold to session lows, slightly below $0.7055 in Europe. Nearby support is seen in the $0.7020-40 area.

EM

• Despite the risk-off mood, the Mexican peso recovered from a four-day low to settle higher on the day. The US dollar reached almost MXN17.96 before being sold slightly below MXN17.68. It has mostly traded between MXN17.50 and MXN18.00 this month and settled a little north of the midpoint. The greenback is trading with a firmer bias and has approached MXN17.83 in Europe. The Brazilian real gained slightly more than the Mexican peso yesterday (~0.85 vs. ~0.65). The two led Latam currencies higher yesterday.

• The dollar turned lower after reaching a three-day high against the offshore yuan, near CNH6.9076 yesterday. As the greenback was sold more broadly, it fell to new session lows around CNH6.8870 in the North American afternoon. It is trading inside yesterday’s range. It settled last week near CNH6.9065. We had thought the dollar fix might be raised this week, but instead, the PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate at a new multi-year low today (CNY6.8898 vs. CNY6.8975 yesterday and CNY6.9007 at the end of last week).

• Indian rupee was sold aggressively. The rupee fell by as much as 1.2% today, the most since February 2022. Foreign selling of assets took a toll and lifted the dollar to a record INR93.76. The INR95 area may be tested next week.

Other Markets

• Asia Pacific equities were mostly lower, and Tokyo markets were on holiday. South Korea’s Kospi bucked the trend and posted a small gain, as have the Indian indices. The Stoxx 600 (STOXX) in Europe is little changed after it fell 2.4% yesterday. US index futures point to a lower opening. The S&P 500 (SPX) settled below its 200-day moving average yesterday for the first time since May 2025.

• Bonds also are under pressure. Benchmark 10-year yields are 4-7 bp higher in Europe, led by the UK Gilts and Italian BTPs. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is up five basis points to 4.30%. The high yield was around 4.32% yesterday, the highest since last August.

• Gold is little changed as it consolidates after the ~$365 loss over the past two sessions. Silver is trading heavier, and it could be the fourth consecutive session of losses of more than $1 an ounce.

• May WTI has been confined to the lower end of yesterday’s range but is near session highs, above $96, ahead of the start of the North American session. Yesterday’s push above $100 briefly marks the week’s high. The May-July Brent futures contracts remain above $100.

Data

• Canada reports January retail sales today. Based on preliminary data, StatsCan tipped a sharp 1.5% gain after a 0.4% decline in December. Excluding auto sales, a 1.2% jump is projected by the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey after a 0.1% rise at the end of last year.

• The UK reported February government finances today, and they were worse than expected. Still, the surge in interest rates renders the data moot. The UK 10-year Gilt yield is up 70 bp since the war began, and the two-year Gilt yield has risen by about 100 bp.

• The eurozone reported January’s trade and current account surpluses of 12.1 bln and 37.9 bln euros, respectively. It is driven by Germany, of course. It is notable that the IMF estimates that Germany’s current account surplus was 5.8% of GDP in 2024 and 4.5% last year. It is projected to rise to 5.1% this year. The IMF estimates that China’s current account surplus was 2.3% of GDP in 2024 and 3.8% in 2025. It forecasts a 2.8% surplus this year. There are critics who say that China’s surplus is larger than the IMF says, but while many IMF projections are debated by private sector economists, they offer a consistent baseline view.

• As widely anticipated, Chinese banks left their one- and five-year loan prime rates steady at 3.0% and 3.5%, respectively. They were last changed in May 2025, when they were lowered by 10 bp.

Original Post

data.title: Yield Of Dreams: A High-Yield Dividend Retirement Portfolio Without The Nightmares
data.description:
When building a portfolio to live off of dividends in retirement, chasing yields seldom works, as it often steers investors into the path of some major landmines that end up blowing up via dividend cuts and major stock price declines. That being said, it is very possible to still build a portfolio of high-dividend stocks that prove to be dependable over time.

However, I do not necessarily advise it for everyone, because in order to do this effectively, you really need to be a capable and willing active investor. High-yielding stocks are generally not the type that you buy and hold indefinitely, but instead, you need to actively monitor and actively manage your portfolio of them. Additionally, I only suggest trying to maximize your retirement income through almost exclusively high-dividend stocks if you absolutely need to generate enough income to retire on. If you have a really large portfolio and can easily live on a dividend yield of 3%, 4%, or 5%, I would personally target a more conservative portfolio, especially if you do not want to be an active investor. If you want to be an active investor in high-yield dividend stocks, and you need to feel comfortable evaluating and managing them, by all means, do so, regardless of your portfolio size.

If you are instead looking for more of a buy-and-hold approach, I think it would be better to focus on some lower-yielding, more sustainable stocks like Realty Income (O), Dividend Kings and Dividend Aristocrats (REGL) (NOBL) like Coca-Cola (KO) and Procter & Gamble (PG), as well as broadly diversified low-cost dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) that has a dividend yield of about 3.5%. However, for those who are pursuing a high-yielding dividend portfolio, I want to provide a few guidelines and individual investment ideas in this article to help you on your journey.

Four Pillars of High-Yield Success

First and foremost, it is important to apply the following four principles when deciding if a stock should belong in a high-yielding portfolio for retirement income:

  1. It needs to generate sufficiently high income so that you can meet your expenses and/or conform to your high-yielding investing strategy. What exactly that number is clearly depends on the individual's security and your personal circumstances.
  2. It must be backed by a durable and defensive business model, and it fits in with your other positions in your portfolio so that you have a well-diversified portfolio across individual sectors.
  3. It must be backed by a solid balance sheet and a reasonably conservative payout ratio such that the dividend appears to be on a sustainable footing.
  4. Finally, the dividend income stream needs to be able to keep up with inflation over time, so it either needs to be generating underlying dividend growth per share and/or generating sufficient surplus income that you can reinvest a portion of it to grow your dividend income by buying more shares over time.

Diversification Beyond Traditional Stocks

With these principles in mind, what are some good elements of a high-yielding dividend portfolio? Well, back to the idea that you need sufficient diversification and durable defensive business models, I think it is important to make sure that you are holding a fairly wide variety of securities from a fairly wide variety of sectors. Fortunately, this can be done while still generating high yields.

For example, you can invest in baby bonds and even some low-investment-grade or high-yield corporate bonds (HYG) that can help to boost your income while being fairly conservative. If you can diversify effectively or even invest in a bond fund, this could serve you quite well. The same applies for preferreds (PFF), where you can get some pretty quality preferreds or even preferred ETFs.

Additional sectors are the business development company space, where you can get high single-digit to low double-digit yields that should be fairly sustainable if you are effective in your selection and active in your portfolio management. Then, of course, there are midstream master limited partnerships, especially if you do not mind dealing with a K-1, or alternatively, you could buy CEFs and ETFs from the sector that can give you a high single-digit or even a low double-digit yield.

You can also buy real estate investment trusts, some of which can yield fairly high amounts of dividends, or even some CEFs and ETFs that have attractive yields. Infrastructure (IFRA) is another sector where you can find some attractive and sustainable yields both through individual stock picks and through funds. Alternative asset managers are not typically high-yielding, but these days, since they are out of favor, you can definitely buy some high-yielding ones, and they combine fairly durable dividends that are backed by long-dated fee-based income streams with attractive growth potential over the long term, especially if they can overcome their current market bearish sentiment.

Additionally, covered call ETFs (JEPI), if used prudently, can be valuable portfolio diversifiers that also generate significant income. Finally, certain precious metals (GLD) leases and ETFs can also generate attractive income and further diversify a portfolio.

Top Picks for Sustainable High Income

As for specific names from these sectors, there are some pretty attractive baby bonds in the BDC space, like the Trinity Capital (TRIN) baby bonds (TRINI), along with several others in that space and beyond, which should be quite sustainable and offer attractive high single-digit yields. Alternatively, you can buy actively managed bond funds, like the Infrastructure Capital Bond Income ETF (BNDS), that yield 8% on a trailing 12-month basis, pay dividends monthly, are actively managed, and have grown their dividends slowly over time, making an attractive combination of high yield and at least some income growth.

In the preferred space, you can also buy attractive individual preferreds, including names like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners preferreds (BIP.PR.A) (BIP.PR.B), among several others, or you can buy actively managed preferred funds, like the Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA). It is similar to BNDS except for preferreds. It is actively managed and, in this case, uses a bit of leverage. PFFA also pays dividends monthly and has a history of growing its dividends at a slight rate each year. Thanks to its leverage and higher risk profile than BNDS due to investing in preferreds, PFFA currently yields 9.8% on a trailing 12-month basis.

In the BDC space, you can buy a passively managed ETF like the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) and an actively managed ETF like the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC), or you can buy blue-chip BDCs at attractive valuations, such as Ares Capital (ARCC), which today yields 10.5%, or Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which today yields 12.7%. If you want to be a little bit less aggressive, you can invest in a name like Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) that has a mid-single-digit base dividend yield, which extends to around 7.8% if you include supplemental dividends.

In the midstream MLP space, there are proven names like Energy Transfer (ET) and MPLX (MPLX) that provide attractive yields north of 7% that should grow at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time while also providing tax-deferred income. Alternatively, you can invest in ETFs like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), the GlobalX MLP ETF (MLPA), or the NEOS MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI), all of which pay out high yields and give K-1-free diversification within the sector.

In the REIT space, it is a bit harder to find a high yield, as the standard passively managed ETF, Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), only yields 3.8% on a trailing 12-month basis. However, if you invest in funds like the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), you can get monthly payouts, a track record of outperformance over the long term, and an 8.6% dividend yield. Meanwhile, in the infrastructure space, some of my favorite funds include the Reeves Utility Income Fund (UTG) and the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), both of which have mid- to high single-digit yields with monthly payouts and well-diversified portfolios, as well as fairly attractive long-term total return track records.

Individual names in the space that I like include Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) (BIPC), Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) (BEPC), and Clearway Energy (CWEN) (CWEN.A), which offer mid-single-digit yields and attractive dividend growth profiles.

The alternative asset management space has pulled back sharply recently, which has created a plethora of attractive opportunities, including names like Blackstone (BX) and Ares Management (ARES), both of which yield north of 5%, as well as others in the sector that also offer attractive yields.

Finally, when it comes to covered call ETFs, it is important to know exactly why you are adding them. I, personally, think that the most useful reason to hold them is for diversification to sectors that normally do not generate much income at all. For example, mega-cap tech is a large and dynamic sector that generally does not pay out much in the way of dividends. If you invest in a fund like the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), or the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), you can get some pretty attractive income paid monthly while still having underlying exposure to the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ).

Another sector that might be worth doing covered calls with is precious metals via funds like the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI). However, I think an even better way to generate income from precious metals is through investing in a diversified pool of leases where you fully own the underlying bullion without the upside being capped at all. You can earn a mid-single-digit yield on that lease investment as well.

Investor Takeaway

This strategy is not risk-free at all and does require active management, as I say at the beginning, because some of these individual holdings may begin to run into trouble or sector allocations may end up out of alignment based on market volatility. Regular monitoring and active management of a portfolio like this are particularly useful.

Additionally, if you have a high-holding stock that appreciates a lot and another stock that becomes heavily discounted, capital recycling driven by valuations and fundamentals can be a great way to enhance returns and income growth over time. In fact, this is a strategy that I think is particularly well-suited to high-yield portfolios, as I detailed here.

Overall, however, if you can successfully diversify and head off potential dividend cuts when problems first emerge, this can be an extremely rewarding strategy for any investor, but especially retirees looking to live off of dividends. You can generate a lot more passive income from your investments than you would be able to by simply investing in a passive dividend-paying ETF, while still being able to keep the risk of dividend cuts and long-term total return on underperformance at bay by using effective portfolio management strategies. This is exactly how we run our portfolios that yield between 7 and 8% and have significantly outperformed over the long term while having below-market beta at High Yield Investor.

data.title: U.S. Lower And Middle Classes Shrinking Over Time As More Households Earn Higher Incomes
data.description:
The ranks of lower- and middle-class households in the United States are thinning. The reason why is remarkable: more households are earning higher incomes, allowing them to move up into the top ranks of the nation's income spectrum.

You don't have to take our word for it. We've organized the U.S. Census Bureau's inflation-adjusted data for household income from 1967 through 2024 into three groups. The first group contains households with annual total money income of $49,999 or less, which represents lower income-earning households. The second group contains households earning between $50,000 and $149,999 to represent middle income-earning households. The third group contains all households earning $150,000 or more.

The following chart confirms the percentage share of lower- and middle-class households in the U.S. is shrinking as the percentage of upper-class households increases.

U.S. households earning $150,000 or more in inflation-adjusted constant 2024 U.S. dollars have risen from 4.6% of all households to 26.1% from 1967 through 2024. Middle-ranked households earning between $50,000 and $149,999 has fallen from 52.4% to 43.8% of all U.S. households. The lowest-ranked households earning real incomes of $49,999 or less has plunged from accounting for 43.0% of all U.S. households to just 30.2%.

A similar pattern holds for U.S. families. See more commentary on this phenomenon here and here.

Reference

U.S. Census Bureau. Historical Income Tables: Households. Table H-17. Households by Total Money Income, Race, and Hispanic Origin of Householder. [Excel spreadsheet]. 25 August 2025.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: SMCI Hit By Export Scandal
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures rise as Netanyahu eases concerns about the Iran conflict. (00:15) Super Micro (SMCI) falls as co-founder, employee charged in Nvidia chip smuggling case. (00:48)Unilever (UL) in talks to sell its food business to McCormick (MKC). (03:00)

This is an abridged transcript.

Stock index futures are firmly in the green.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) rose 0.90%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) gained 1.02%, and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures (INDU) advanced 0.97%.

Bitcoin is up 1% at $70,000. Gold is up 0.5% at $4,677.

Market sentiment improved after Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu said the country is helping to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Oil prices slipped on the news that Netanyahu said Iran no longer has the capacity to enrich uranium or make ballistic missiles.

Crude oil is down 0.2% at $95. Brent crude is at $109.

European indexes also rebound after an ease in oil prices.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.2% and the DAX is up 0.6%.

In Asia, the markets in Japan (NKY:IND) closed for a holiday.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is down 22% in premarket action.

Three individuals linked to the AI server maker, including a co-founder, were charged with violating export laws by assisting in the smuggling of at least $2.5 billion worth of U.S. AI technology to China.

The Justice Department did not name Super ‌Micro in the complaint, referring only to a "U.S. manufacturer."

The company said it was informed by federal prosecutors of the indictment on Thursday. It noted that it was not named as a defendant in the case and said it had cooperated with investigators.

In an indictment unsealed on Thursday, the U.S. government alleged that Yih-Shyan “Wally” Liaw, Ruei-Tsan “Steven” Chang and Ting-Wei “Willy” Sun worked together to violate the Export Control Reform Act. Liaw co-founded Super ​Micro in 1993, and joined its board of directors in 2023. Chang was a sales manager in the Taiwan office of Super Micro, while Sun was ⁠a contractor.

U.S. officials allege the trio went to great lengths to hide their actions from both U.S.-based server manufacturers and export control authorities, even using hair dryers to remove labels and serial numbers ​from the real machines and placing them on dummy machines left behind after the real machines had been shipped to China.

The efforts have yielded around $2.5 billion in sales for the server maker since 2024, with $510 million sold between late April 2025 and mid-May 2025 going to the Southeast Asian company and on to China, the indictment said. The plaintiff said the server maker had no U.S. Commerce Department license to export servers featuring Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to China.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) said it placed its co-founder and the sales manager on leave and terminated its ties with the contractor, after being made aware of the charges on Thursday.

The attorney’s office said the co-founder and contractor were both arrested on Thursday, while the sales manager is a fugitive.

Unilever (UL) announced that it received an inbound offer for its food business and is in discussions with McCormick (MKC).

The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter, that an all-stock deal may be announced within weeks if talks don't fall apart. The exact structure remains unknown.

Unilever's (UL) food business, which houses brands such as Knorr and Hellmann's, could be worth tens of billions of dollars. McCormick's (MKC) products include Frank's RedHot sauce and French's yellow mustard.

Separating its food business would enable Unilever (UL) to focus on its beauty, personal care and home divisions. The company spun off its ice cream business Magnum last year.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Yes, Everything Is More Volatile
data.description:
By Mike Larson

No, it’s not your imagination. Everything is more volatile in 2026 - it’s just a question of degree. And that means you have to adjust your trading strategies.

Check out the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the year-to-date change in the ICE BofAML MOVE Index (MOVE), the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX), the CBOE Gold Volatility Index (GVZ), and the CBOE Crude Oil Volatility Index (OVX). That covers Treasury bonds, stocks, gold, and oil.

Where the Worst Volatility is... and Isn’t

(Source: TradingView)

No surprise that oil is the big “winner” in the rising-vol race. The OVX has soared 224% so far this year. Equity vol is up more than 65%, while gold vol is up 33% and bond vol is up 30%. If you’re looking for a port in the volatility storm, you’re not finding it right now.

Some of the swings from day-to-day... or even hour-to-hour... have been breathtaking. While "headline" risk is the worst in the energy patch given the Middle East conflict, bond prices are also fluctuating more aggressively due to rapid shifts in monetary policy outlooks. And precious metals had a wild start to 2026 after they essentially became "meme-i-fied."

My advice? Play your cards closer to the vest if you’re an active trader. Trade smaller or less often. Or if you’re a longer-term investor with time on your side, take advantage of short-term dislocations to build positions on the cheap.

I can’t tell you what the next headline is going to say. But I can tell you that the technical and fundamental backdrop is weaker in 2026 than 2025. The data shows it. Ignoring that reality could be hazardous to your financial health.

data.title: Four Corners Property Trust: This REIT's Growth Story Advances Despite Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT), I highlighted their attractive dividend yield backed by solid financials and significant growth potential given their acquisitions and potential to deliver solid lease spreads over the following years thanks to broader macro tailwinds.

With the company's significant growth continuing and adding over 100 new properties last year while the valuation continues to be attractive alongside their solid and sustainable dividend, FCPT remains a Buy despite the ongoing macro uncertainty.

A Mixed Quarter, But Growth Advances

FCPT reported a mixed Q4, with a miss on revenue but FFO in line with the market's expectations, ending the year with a near record ~$318 million in acquisitions across 105 properties, while the AFFO grew to a strong $183.41 million in 2025 (vs. $162.76 million in 2024), meaning a decent ~2.89% increase to $1.78 per share (vs. $1.73).

They also mentioned an interesting aspect to remember regarding their strong streak of acquisitions (with a weighted average cash yield of 6.8% plus a weighted average remaining lease term of 12.2 years as of the end of Q4 2025), as funding it through issuing stock at accretive dividend levels enhances the company's financials and places them in a solid position going forward, especially if debt costs fall:

We funded acquisitions primarily through equity issued at accretive pricing which lowers our leverage profile and provides us with an over-equitized capital position and significant leverage capacity entering 2026.

In fact, the company's outlook shows this, with the CEO expecting to pivot towards debt going forward for growth in 2026, targeting increased acquisition activity (high chance for a record, I might add), continuing their diversification efforts, with a diverse set of five properties acquired in March 2026 at the moment of writing this, mentioning the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

So today, our balance sheet is over-equitized. I'll repeat that. Today, our balance sheet is overequitized with net leverage near 5x. Further, we didn't raise debt when we would have acquired a 7%-plus coupon. Now we can access much more favorable debt capital markets with a coupon rate in the 4.5% to 5.5% range, depending on the structure and term, whether term loans or notes. This is much more attractive given where we see cap rates today.

Financially, based on FCPT's latest report, we continue to see a solid position, with a manageable amount of debt that's covered by their assets, with a well-laddered maturity profile and only $50 million due in 2026, while their leverage reached 4.9x in Q4, slightly below their target of 5.0x to 6.0x.

Regarding the dividend, they announced a decent 3.2% increase previously, getting us to a solid ~5.76% yield today, representing ~$156 million, for a payout ratio of roughly 85%, which is not the best out there, but it's fine overall given the yield and growth they can deliver.

Macro Uncertainty Rising

Although we're talking about a company with properties that are very far from the ongoing Iran conflict, it's important to note how this is not a local issue, as it has visible implications on vital commodities for every economy.

The significant disruptions and spike in prices can have deeply negative effects on the US and the global economy as a whole, as they can lead to inflation and a slowdown, while the fundamentals of oil were already relatively weak, especially when it comes to demand, with the IEA even downgrading its demand forecasts as recently as February and again in March.

Especially with the Fed's latest decision to keep rates steady on the back of the ongoing Middle East uncertainty and expecting a single cut later this year, adding even more fear and pressure to an already bad environment.

Due to this type of uncertainty and inflation worries, we see US stocks continue their drop while treasury yields remain elevated, with this conflict leading to a spike that wiped out months of progress, as the market has to take into account much more than just the Fed's decisions, ultimately demanding a better risk-adjusted reward despite the current retreat to the dollar due to the ongoing fear and unrest.

As mentioned before, being a REIT means that this company comes with significant exposure to the environment, as that can affect their borrowing costs directly and the creditworthiness of their tenants, with the ongoing consumer weakness already taking its toll on the industries they are exposed to, with problems seen especially in foodservice as affordability concerns have been rising since these companies were also affected by tariffs on top of demand issues.

Even if the Iran conflict was over quickly and the global economy recovered, we shouldn't ignore the other points of pressure seen in the US, including the weak consumer economy affecting the company's tenants, relatively high costs of debt, and the uncertainty surrounding Japan's potential US treasury sell-off, which is a major risk for the country and its borrowing costs.

They also talked about the ongoing trends seen in the broader real estate market, with rising construction costs and weak demand pressuring new real estate developments, which can be a major advantage given the upcoming lease expirations, potentially allowing them to gain significantly from favorable negotiations.

Risks Reiterated

Although I believe this is also an opportunity, the fact that a significant amount of the company's leases mature over the coming ~8 years (especially 2027-2033 thanks to the Darden spin-off lease maturities) can also be a risk in case the environment deteriorates, with the company risking a significant maturity expiration wall in the face of a potential downturn, making it harder to find tenants if the environment becomes bad enough.

This would come on top of the company's pivot towards issuing debt, as they said, with an increase in leverage during a time like this being quite risky, especially if we see a higher-for-longer rate environment that can affect their refinancing activity over the next few years.

Valuation

For FCPT's DCF to Equity valuation, starting from their reported $183.41 million AFFO in 2025, I'll assume a 5.5% CAGR until 2030 this time (meaning ~$193.50 million in 2026) to highlight the company's significant investments and potential to benefit from macro improvements at a time when their lease mature (great potential for better spreads), followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a 10% discount rate given the macro pressure, as well as 106.54 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.91 billion, for an intrinsic value of $27.32 per share, which continues to be above the current $25.32 at the moment of writing this, despite using relatively conservative estimates that the company can certainly beat given their ramp-up and potential macro improvements.

Conclusion

FCPT remains a buy, backed by a discounted valuation, solid growth potential, and an attractive dividend yield, standing to benefit from potential macro tailwinds and significant investments.

This isn't a risk-free investment, of course, as we're talking about a REIT that can see significant pressure from the ongoing macro uncertainty and high tenant concentration, but the overall risk-reward continues to be in their favor, in my opinion.

Performance Recap

In the fourth quarter of 2025, the PGIM Floating Rate Income Fund Class Z (FRFZX) returned 2.39% net of fees and outperformed its benchmark, the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index, gross of fees.

Key Contributors

  • Security selection in health care & pharmaceutical.
  • Security selection in chemicals.

Key Detractors

  • Security selection in gaming & lodging & leisure.
  • Security selection in media & entertainment.

Market Review

U.S. leveraged loans continued to generate positive total returns in the fourth quarter, driving 2025 performance to approximately 6%, in-line with our expectations for the year.

Gross loan issuance totaled $171.3 billion in the quarter, with net new supply reaching approximately $49.7 billion as repricing and refinancing activity continued to comprise the bulk of activity. For the year, $984.3 billion of gross supply came to market, while net supply was $198.8 billion.

By quality, BB-rated loans outperformed, outpacing B-rated loans on positive performance and CCC-rated loans, which declined during the quarter.

The average price of all loans within the Index ended Q4 at 96.55, slightly below 97 at the beginning of the quarter. Meanwhile, spreads widened by 16 bps as yields ended the period at 7.73%.

Leveraged loan retail fund flows reversed course in Q4, with $4.4 billion exiting the asset class, erasing the $4.4 billion of inflows during Q3. As such, loan retail fund flows ended the year positive at $2.2 billion.

Meanwhile, collateralized loan obligation (CLO) formation remained robust at $124.2 billion in the fourth quarter, bringing 2025 CLO issuance to $526.5 billion.

Barron's: PGIM Investments ranked 4 out of 48, 8 out of 47, 12 out of 46 firms for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods ended 12/31/2024, respectively. See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. PGIM Floating Rate Income Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the Loan Participation Funds category were: 20/235, 57/214, 3/204, and 3/162, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund's respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2025: 80/239, 8/219, 39/205, and 7/170, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Despite elevated interest rates, stable credit fundamentals and a lack of near-term maturities continued to keep defaults manageable. The par-weighted loan default rate, including distressed exchanges, ended December at 2.87%, down from 3.49% in September.

Quarter

Overall security selection contributed the most to performance during the quarter, with selection in health care & pharmaceutical, chemicals, and paper & packaging contributing the most. This was partially offset by selection in gaming & lodging & leisure, media & entertainment, and midstream energy, which detracted.

Overall sector allocation detracted from performance during the quarter, with an underweight to the U.S. bank loan sector, and overweights to the investment grade corporates and Euro-denominated bank loans sectors detracting the most. This was partially offset by an overweight to CLO AA bonds, which contributed.

Overall industry allocation detracted from performance in the fourth quarter, with an overweight to chemicals, and underweights in technology and retailers & restaurants detracting the most. This was partially offset by overweights in banking and cable & satellite, and an underweight to automotive, which contributed.

From a market perspective, having more risk in the portfolio, on average, over the period than the relevant benchmark contributed to performance.

Portfolio Outlook

We expect a similar performance outcome in 2026, with returns in the 5.5-6% range, driven primarily by carry, but tempered by downward pressure from lower base rates, continued repricing transactions, elevated credit concerns, and potential technical pressures if net issuance accelerates. That said, we do not expect broad-based deterioration in fundamentals, but defaults and liability management exercises (LME) should remain elevated with recoveries below historical averages.

For 2026, we remain cautious on the lower-quality tail of the market, particularly on credits impacted by recent LMEs where recoveries and outcomes remain uncertain. In the primary market, net issuance should remain manageable, but risks of a higher supply include mounting datacenter-related financing, rising M&A activity, and more private-to-public transactions. Longer-term, the 2028 maturity wall looms, with approximately $550 billion of maturities, including approximately $168 billion rated B3 or lower. By the second half of 2026, these issuers are likely to face increased scrutiny and pricing pressure amid elevated liquidity and refinancing concerns.

data.title: Broadcom Or Marvell? Choosing Between Stability And Aggressive Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Chip technology is crucial for the further development of AI infrastructure, such as data centers; yet, beyond their availability, there are a number of requirements that must be met to make computing processes more cost-effective and energy-efficient. The custom chips offered by the two industry giants, Marvell Technology (MRVL) and Broadcom (AVGO), are cheaper and more efficient in terms of energy consumption per watt in data centers. As the barrier to entry in this market is enormous, oligopoly between the two companies will persist, creating additional dividends for scaling their businesses and improving their financial results. Despite their similarities, MRVL and AVGO have different "genetic codes" and business strategies, making it interesting to compare them. I want to find out which of these companies looks the most attractive for implementing an investment strategy to capitalize on the "new wave" of the AI supercycle. I will try to cover all the key points in this article so each reader can find the answer that works best for them.

The Economic Landscape of the Custom Chip Market

This market has entered a phase of "mature growth," with the early era of shortages giving way to a period of optimization and expansion of production to meet the needs of data centers. Analysing the growth rates of the custom chip market, note that this semiconductor segment is still far from saturation. Based on the latest data, ASIC's global market was valued at $22.6 billion in 2025, and the average annual growth rate is 10.26%. Meanwhile, it's expected to grow to $44.8 billion by 2033, with the same average annual growth rate.

A key driver of growth in the custom chip market is total cost of ownership, since ASICs offer greater cost-effectiveness for the basic solutions required by data center operators than the more expensive GPUs. Cost optimization is also driven by the fact that data centers are facing rising energy costs for computing operations. Results from assessments of custom chip energy efficiency show that ASICs are 30-40% more power-efficient than general-purpose chips. Also worth noting is that many hyperscalers have accumulated sufficient expertise to write their own software tailored to their hardware, making it possible to transition to ASICs as a result of reduced computational complexity.

Prospects for the economic outlook of the custom chip market depend on the expansion of TSMC's production capacity, where 3 nm production is already fully booked through 2026, and competition continues to intensify around 2 nm (its mass production is scheduled for the second half of 2026). It's important to produce 2-nm chips since they're denser and smaller, meaning you can pack more "brains" into the same chip size. This results in a product that runs faster while consuming less energy. With the market entry barrier having risen to $1 billion, an oligopoly has been created for AVGO and MRVL, protecting them from competition by external players. The importance of TSMC to the development of the custom chip market stems from its role of acting as a manufacturing base. To give an example, MRVL and AVGO send them "blueprints" (functioning as a think tank that offers hyperscalers the design of an ideal chip for AI processes), based on which printing takes place on very expensive machines at the TSMC factory. A finished microchip is the result of this partnership.

Nevertheless, even with this protective barrier, there are two factors that could lead to increased competition: vertical integration and the Chinese factor. In terms of vertical integration, businesses such as OpenAI and Anthropic have begun reducing their reliance on Nvidia (NVDA) by partnering with AVGO to develop their own chips. While this means new orders and revenue growth for Broadcom, this partnership could eventually result in the creation of new custom chip players capable of covering their needs through their own developments. This will require a significant effort, however, and it is not certain that it will succeed (it is also not certain that such companies are interested in creating competition for AVGO, since remaining partners would be more economically advantageous and cheaper due to large-scale CapEx).

As part of the Chinese factor, a significant increase in the production of domestically manufactured 7nm/5nm chips is worth noting. Although technologically outdated and more power-hungry, these chips are creating an oversupply in the ASIC segment, where they are needed for simple AI and mining systems. This has resulted in falling prices for custom chips in the lower-end segment. Nonetheless, on the global custom chip market, it will be those who offer products with higher engineering efficiency who come out on top. This is because it is important not only to have a fast chip but also to ensure it consumes as little energy as possible under the same load.

Overview of MRVL's Strategy

MRVL transformed itself into a highly specialized data infrastructure provider in 2026, with a focus on resolving data center bottlenecks. Its business strategy is built on a "cloud first" approach. It has enabled the company to divest non-core assets (the sale of automotive Ethernet device manufacturing assets for $2.5 billion) to focus entirely on data center services. Based on the financial statements, this data center segment accounts for 74% of total revenue ($6.10 billion), while a year earlier it was 72%. Contrary to GPU manufacturer NVDA, Marvell offers hyperscalers services to develop their own AI processors with 30-40% higher energy efficiency-a critical factor for reducing data center TCO.

Furthermore, it aims to address the primary bottleneck in performance: optical interconnects. March 2026 saw the unveiling of the first 1.6 Tbps optical DSP platform. The technology enables a doubling of bandwidth, the gold standard for high-speed optical modules. Since acquiring Celestial AI for $3.25 billion, MRVL has been using its flagship product, Photonic Fabric. The goal is to improve connectivity in data centers, expanding technological capabilities and opportunities to secure new contracts with hyperscalers building infrastructure based on ASICs. In fact, in the context of developing the use of photonics to improve optical connections, there may be another industry player of interest to MRVL: POET Technologies (POET). If you want to learn why POET might be a potential acquisition target for MRVL, click on the link to read my recent article on the role of POET's technologies in the Photonic Fabric product.

Overview of AVGO's Strategy

Fast forward to 2026, and AVGO has become a full-fledged technology conglomerate of the AI era, dominating the global ASIC market. Apart from making custom AI chips, it's carved out a competitive niche in the network infrastructure segment, allowing it to use a more hybrid growth strategy than MRVL. By various estimates, AVGO's share of the custom chip segment reaches 60-80%. Its customer portfolio includes five major clients, whose total orders amount to a record $73 billion. This includes $53 billion in orders for custom chips. The strategic goal is to achieve $100 billion in revenue from custom chip sales by 2027.

As part of the network infrastructure segment, AGVO presented a range of products. Firstly, it began mass shipments of a 102.4 Tbps switch, doubling the bandwidth of the previous generation of switches. Secondly, the company made a breakthrough in optical technology following the launch of the Taurus platform, providing a transition to 1.6T and 3.2T connections. Thirdly, it is developing synergies with VMware, enabling a transition to a subscription model and ensuring stable cash flow generation. Its economic significance stems from the fact that the software gross margin has reached 93%, allowing for the generation of massive FCF (just $8 billion in Q1 2026).

Key Differences Between MRVL and AVGO

Even though MRVL and AVGO both specialize in manufacturing custom chips, in many ways, fundamental analysis of their business strategies reveals them to be players with fundamentally different approaches. For starters, they're different in scale, making Broadcom look like the dominant player in the global custom chip market, whereas Marvell appears to be a player trying to catch up quickly, with a focus on niche optical solutions. According to their business models, AVGO is a confident conglomerate (particularly after the acquisition of VMware), while MRVL is a highly specialized semiconductor player (lacking the portfolio diversification cushion that AVGO has with its software business). As part of its customer portfolio, AVGO has orders from all major hyperscalers (as its production scale allows it to fulfill any order), while MRVL also collaborates with secondary players (exhibiting flexibility in relationships and adapting more quickly to the specific needs of niche customers).

In networking technologies, AVGO dominates the switching market, since their Tomahawk 6 chips are the industry gold standard. MRVL has competitive advantages in optical interconnects, which use PAM4 technology to handle a significant portion of the signal transmission between racks. In terms of financial profiles, it is worth noting that AVGO is a more stable player, which generates high FCF with a margin exceeding 40%. It allows the company to increase shareholder returns in the long term and carry out a share buyback program worth tens of billions of dollars. MRVL is in a more modest position, investing capital in R&D. Considering that the company's liquidity is lower than its short-term liabilities, it has significantly lower financial stability. An additional share issuance is possible instead of a share buyback.

Nevertheless, a difference exists between the companies in terms of their potential valuation, which favors MRVL over AVGO, as the latter's shares are trading at a significant premium. Taking forward EV/sales and EV/EBITDA multiples, the premium of AVGO over MRVL stands at 104.28% and 8.87%, correspondingly. However, at current EV/EBITDA levels, this premium is also high at 38.83%. My opinion is that it is precisely this valuation gap that creates greater potential for Marvell's market value in 2026. Furthermore, a focus on solutions in the custom chip segment (no additional diversification) allows the company to concentrate on the rapidly growing AI industry segment, generate new orders from hyperscalers, and scale up production.

Risks of Investing in MRVL and AVGO

Even though the semiconductor sector continues to grow at a rapid pace, by 2026 the risks associated with investing in custom chip manufacturers will increase, in my opinion because of the general tension surrounding issues such as the "AI bubble," "inflation risks," and "trade wars." General market risks, along with specific threats to MRVL and AVGO, deserve special attention.

Among the general market risks, a major threat is the economic viability of the record-breaking capital expenditures planned by the largest hyperscalers. Should these investments stall or the return on investment ROI from AI implementation fall short of expectations, market capitalization could decline for all companies reliant on the continuation of the AI supercycle. The natural threat to MRVL and AVGO is the risk of overproduction, since the previous two years can be characterized as a phase of "rapid growth." In connection with overproduction, there may be a price correction, because the supply will be so saturated that there will be no ASIC shortage to speak of. It is also important not to overlook geopolitical factors, due to which there may be an increase in the localization of chip production in the U.S., leading to restrictive measures on the export of custom chips to China and other regional markets. That would narrow the list of buyers, adversely affecting the valuation of chip manufacturers.

The risks facing MRVL are more specific and reflect the fact that the company is smaller in scale than AVGO. MRVL derives over 74% of its revenue from the data center segment, where it has a narrow list of clients. Even the refusal of a single hyperscaler to use custom chips could significantly worsen the company's financial results. Furthermore, the future performance of MRVL depends on the success of the integration of assets acquired in 2026 (Celestial AI and XConn). Failure to meet expectations due to delays would postpone the launch of new products, providing AVGO with a competitive advantage. What I don't like about the company's financial profile is the low quick ratio, because short-term liabilities amount to $3.22 billion, and cash reserves stand at $2.64 billion. It makes it possible for MRVL to raise additional capital, like through a new share issue or a bond offering.

Risks for AVGO are less specific because the company is large-scale, which could become a problem due to potential antitrust scrutiny. With a market share of over 60%, the dominance of AVGO in the ASIC segment could attract additional attention from regulators. That said, I am currently less convinced that such action will be taken, considering how critical the stable development of the semiconductor sector is to the U.S. economy. A regulatory battle could lead to a decline in the production of a critically important product. More pressing is the risk associated with the VMware integration, given that AVGO has already encountered a critical vulnerability in VMware's software, leading to reputational damage and a slowdown in software sales growth. This is why, by 2026, the share price had fallen by 8.72%, whereas MRVL's rose by 3.18%. In this way, regarding the issue of the AI supercycle's continuation, it appears that AVGO is more resilient, and MRVL is more suited to realizing the potential of aggressive market growth. But the fragile balance and lack of diversification make Marvell's business profile less resilient.

Conclusion

Based on an analysis of the fundamental value and growth strategies of the two largest custom chip manufacturers, MRVL and AVGO, my conclusion is that, although they share the same industry focus, both assets are suited to different types of investors. MRVL shares allow investors to bet on aggressive growth with a higher multiple increase in market capitalization. However, the risk profile here is higher than that of AVGO. Broadcom is more suitable for those who tend to choose more stable assets with "blue-chip" status. However, AVGO has a more stable business and financial profile, and its shares are more overvalued. MRVL is trading at a discount to AVGO on a number of key multiples, which makes it more attractive to me personally. That's why, although I rate both companies as "Buy," Marvell is my pick.

data.title: BUZZ Investing: Geopolitical Shocks Trigger Broad Market Retreat
data.description:
U.S. equity markets shifted from a sector rotation driven by AI disruption fears to a broad sell-off fueled by geopolitical shocks in Iran and a surprisingly weak jobs report, raising stagflation concerns.

Market movements discussed below reflect a range of factors, and specific drivers are based on market observations and may not fully explain performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Other investments may have performed differently during the same period.

U.S. equity markets transitioned from a tactical rotation to a broader, more defensive retrenchment during the recent period between selection dates (February 11, 2026 – March 12, 2026, the “Period”). During the latter half of February, the "SaaS-pocalypse" narrative intensified, as investors aggressively de-rated enterprise software leaders like Salesforce, Atlassian, and Workday over fears that autonomous AI agents would disrupt traditional per-seat licensing models. Leadership rotated toward financials, industrials, healthcare, and select cyclical segments as last year’s dominant mega-cap names lagged. Some coined this dynamic an “immaculate rotation,” reflecting the market’s ability to reallocate capital away from crowded leadership without materially disrupting index-level stability. However, this relative index-level stability proved fleeting as the Period progressed, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite ultimately declining, erasing earlier year-to-date gains. Against this backdrop, the BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index (the “BUZZ Index”) exhibited relative resilience, declining 1.4% compared with declines of 3.7% and 3.2% in the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite, respectively.

The market’s tone shifted in early March, as a confluence of macro shocks triggered a shift from sector-specific dispersion to broad-based liquidation. The February 28 coordinated military strikes by the U.S. and Israel on Iranian targets introduced a sudden wave of geopolitical instability, resulting in a surge in Brent crude prices amid shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. This inflationary shock was compounded by the March 6 Department of Labor report, which revealed a larger-than-expected contraction of 92,000 jobs and a rise in the unemployment rate to 4.4%, the highest in nearly two years. These dual shocks effectively collapsed the "soft landing" narrative, replacing it with fears of stagflation and a potential "policy trap" for the Federal Reserve. As risk-off sentiment took hold, safe-haven assets like gold and silver reached new highs while speculative appetite vanished, leaving equities to digest a high-correlation sell-off as the Period concluded near its session lows.

The BUZZ Index returned -7.03% during the month of February compared to a return of -0.76% for the S&P 500 Index during the same period. Year-to-date, the BUZZ Index lags the S&P 500 with returns of -5.32% and 0.68%, respectively, as of the end of February.

Netflix and Nebius Lead BUZZ Gains on Strategic Developments

Shares of Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) were among the leading contributors to BUZZ Index performance during the Period. The stock advanced following the company’s decision to withdraw from its proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery, allowing Paramount Skydance to pursue the transaction while collecting a $2.8 billion breakup fee. Investors appeared to view the outcome favorably, as it removed the need for Netflix to assume significant incremental debt and regulatory uncertainty associated with a complex media merger. The move also reinforced management’s longstanding preference for organic growth and disciplined capital allocation. With the potential distraction of a large-scale acquisition removed, market attention returned to Netflix’s core strengths, including its global content production engine and established track record of generating high engagement across its platform.

Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) was another notable contributor, rising following the announcement of a strategic partnership with Nvidia that includes a planned $2 billion investment to support the deployment of artificial intelligence infrastructure. The agreement is expected to enable Nebius to expand its AI cloud capabilities and deploy large-scale Nvidia systems as demand for accelerated computing continues to grow. Nebius has positioned itself as an emerging provider of AI-focused cloud capacity, and the endorsement from Nvidia was interpreted by investors as a meaningful validation of the company’s technology and growth strategy. Shares responded positively to the announcement, reflecting broader investor interest in companies building the infrastructure required to support the expanding AI ecosystem.

AI Infrastructure and Fintech Weakness Weigh on BUZZ Performance

Applied Digital Corp. (APLD) was among the largest detractors from BUZZ Index performance during the Period, as investor sentiment toward AI infrastructure operators softened. Shares declined amid broader volatility across the emerging “neocloud” segment following reports that financing challenges had surfaced around a large data-center development associated with CoreWeave. While the situation did not directly involve Applied Digital, the news appeared to raise broader questions about the availability and cost of capital required to fund large-scale GPU clusters and next-generation data centers. Additional pressure followed disclosures that Nvidia had exited its equity stake in the company during the fourth quarter, a move that some investors interpreted cautiously despite Nvidia’s history of actively rotating its investment portfolio. Taken together, the developments highlighted the sensitivity of capital-intensive AI infrastructure businesses to changes in credit conditions and investor expectations around funding timelines.

SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI) was another notable detractor during the Period as shares continued a difficult start to the year. The stock had declined meaningfully year-to-date, with weakness appearing to reflect a combination of valuation sensitivity, high beta exposure, and a moderation in retail-driven momentum that had previously supported the shares. Some analysts suggested that positioning rather than fundamental deterioration may have been a key factor, as the broader digital banking segment also experienced pressure. During the Period, CEO Anthony Noto purchased approximately $1 million of stock in the open market, a move that historically has been interpreted by some investors as a signal of confidence in the company’s longer-term outlook, even as the shares faced near-term volatility.

BUZZ Index March 2026 Rebalance Highlights

RTX Corporation

After weeks of escalating rhetoric and military positioning, the United States announced on March 1 that it had carried out missile strikes targeting Iran’s leadership. The operation reportedly targeted senior Iranian leadership, according to media reports, and was intended to destabilize the current regime and halt Iran’s nuclear weapons ambitions. While the initial reaction in equity markets was relatively muted, sector-level impacts soon began to emerge. Oil prices moved sharply higher after the Strait of Hormuz, which runs along Iran’s southern coast and handles nearly 20% of global daily oil traffic, was effectively shut down. The U.S. defense sector also rallied, as military conflicts historically tend to increase demand for defense spending. RTX Corp. (RTX), one of the largest U.S. defense contractors, saw a notable surge in investor sentiment following the onset of the strikes. Sentiment continued to climb as the month progressed after it became increasingly clear that the conflict was unlikely to quickly resolve. This month, RTX enters the BUZZ with a 1.22% weight.

Block, Inc.

On February 26, Block, Inc. (XYZ), led by former Twitter founder Jack Dorsey, reported earnings alongside a sweeping restructuring announcement. The company revealed plans to lay off roughly 4,000 employees, approximately 40% of its workforce, and transition many of those functions to AI-driven teams. While investors have long expected artificial intelligence to gradually replace certain human tasks, the speed and scale of Block’s move was a shock for many. Management framed the restructuring as a major efficiency initiative aimed at lowering costs and streamline operations through automation. The market’s reaction was overwhelmingly positive, with shares jumping 20% the following day. Investor sentiment also surged, as many retail investors pointed to the move as a clear example of real-world AI adoption beginning to reshape companies. XYZ joins the BUZZ Index in March with a 0.69% weight.
